studentQuestionArray.content: An infant has abdominal pain, blood-filled stools, vomiting, and tachycardia. The health-care provider instructs the radiologist to perform a barium enema test. Which condition does the nurse expect will be confirmed?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Necrotizing enterocolitis is the damage to the intestinal tract that occurs in premature infants. Abdominal x-rays and stool collected for occult blood are used to diagnose necrotizing enterocolitis.
Option 2:Intussusception is a medical condition in which parts of the intestine hold on to adjacent parts of intestine. A barium enema is used to provide clear imaging of the colon and identify intussusception.
Option 3:Volvulus is abnormal twisting of the gastrointestinal tract that results in bowel obstruction. A complete blood count along with electrolyte measurement helps diagnose volvulus.
Option 4:Pyloric stenosis is the thickening of pyloric muscle and it is diagnosed using ultrasound.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client visits a nurse for preliminary testing. After examination and analysis, the data does not indicate a health problem. Which diagnosis statement will the nurse use?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:If the nurse can legally provide interventions to treat or prevent a problem, it can be stated as a nursing diagnosis.
Option 2:If there is an actual problem, it can be stated as a medical diagnosis.
Option 3:After examination, the nurse did not find any health problem. Therefore, the diagnosis is stated as a wellness diagnosis.
Option 4:If there is a complication of a disease, treatment, or diagnostic procedure, it can be stated as a collaborative problem.

studentQuestionArray.content: An older client complains of severe back pain for 2 months and joint pain after walking a short distance. Which imaging technique will be ordered to measure the client’s bone mineral density?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Computed tomography imaging technique is used to measure the volumes of internal structures. It uses strong radio waves while measuring the bone density and the reading may not be accurate.
Option 2:Magnetic resonance imaging is very expensive, and is impractical for evaluation of bone density of the clients.
Option 3:DEXA scans are used to diagnose osteoporosis and give the accurate measurement of bone mineral density. This imaging technique is preferred because it is fast and noninvasive.
Option 4:Hydrodensitometry or underwater weighing is used to measure the body composition and the percentage of body fat. It is not possible to use this method in children, the elderly, and in older clients who are severely ill, as it requires the client to be immersed in a tank of water.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is reviewing a client’s electrocardiogram (ECG). Which wave represents the ventricular repolarization in an ECG graph?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The P wave on an ECG represents the conduction of an impulse from the SA node.
Option 2:A QRS complex in an ECG graph represents ventricular depolarization.
Option 3:A T wave in an ECG represents the recovery of ventricles, called ventricular repolarization.
Option 4:A U wave is observed in an ECG when there is an electrolyte imbalance or when the client is taking certain medication such as digitalis or epinephrine.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse working in a women’s health clinic is preparing the exam rooms for the clients. Which test helps detect cervical cancer?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:An FSH test helps detect the cause of infertility. This test does not provide information on cervical cancer.
Option 2:A Papanicolaou smear (Pap smear) test helps detect cervical cancer.
Option 3:A HIV test helps detect AIDS.
Option 4:Complete blood count is done as a part of general physical examination. It helps detect various diseases such as anemia, infections, or leukemia.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client visits a clinic with a complaint of abdominal pain, rectal bleeding, and pain while passing stools. The health-care provider suspects colon polyps. Which diagnostic procedure is most useful in detecting the source of the client’s issues?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Sigmoidoscopy is the procedure of examining the rectum and sigmoid colon. This procedure is used in investigating the cause for loss of blood and pain while passing stools. It is used to remove small polyps in the colon and also to screen colorectal cancer.
Option 2:It is used to detect gallstones, fecal impaction, and distended bowel.
Option 3:Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) is a procedure used in examining the upper gastrointestinal tract like lining of the esophagus, stomach, and duodenum.
Option 4:Ultrasonography is used in detecting tissue abnormalities like cysts and edema.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client complains of difficulty in eating food. After examination, the nurse diagnoses the client's problem as "impaired swallowing." Which class of the domain nutrition includes the problem diagnosed in the client?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Digestion is the activity of converting foodstuffs into substances suitable for absorption and assimilation. The client in the given condition is unable to have food. So, it will not come under the class digestion.
Option 2:Ingestion is the activity of taking food into the body. Therefore, the client's problem comes under the class ingestion of nutrition.
Option 3:Absorption is the act of taking up nutrients through body tissues. It does not include problems related to swallowing.
Option 4:Metabolism is the chemical and physical processes that occur in the body to release energy for all vital processes. It does not include problems related to swallowing.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client visits a clinic and complains of weakness and decreased urine output. The client’s blood pressure is high and the nurse suspects the client's kidney function is impaired. Which laboratory test is suitable for assessing the condition of the client?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Rise in blood pressure, decreased urine volume, and weakness may indicate impaired kidney function. Urea is the end product of protein metabolism and is excreted through the kidneys. Increase in levels of serum blood urea nitrogen (BUN) level indicates impaired kidney function.
Option 2:A blood glucose test is performed to diagnose diabetes mellitus in the client.
Option 3:A hemoglobin test is ordered as a part of general physical examination or to diagnose certain conditions like anemia or polycythemia.
Option 4:An albumin test is performed to evaluate liver function. This test is performed when the client has symptoms of jaundice and weight loss.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client complains of pain that starts and stops at periodic intervals. Which descriptor of time does the nurse use in the diagnostic statement?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The nurse will describe the pain as acute pain if the pain lasts less than 6 months.
Option 2:The nurse will describe the pain as chronic pain if the pain lasts more than 6 months.
Option 3:The nurse will describe the pain as continuous pain if the pain is uninterrupted or going on without a stop.
Option 4:The nurse will describe the pain as intermittent pain if the pain stops and starts again at periodic intervals.

studentQuestionArray.content: A mother tells the health-care provider that, when she kisses her child, the child's skin tastes salty. The parent also reports that the child has a cough and shows poor growth and weight loss. Which diagnostic test is recommended for the child?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Salty skin, cough, poor growth, and weight loss indicates cystic fibrosis. It is due to the presence of high amounts of sodium and chloride in the sweat of individuals with cystic fibrosis. Sweat test, which measures sodium and chloride, is the diagnostic test for cystic fibrosis.
Option 2:Pulmonary function test is used to assess the lung function. It measures the lung volumes and flow rates.
Option 3:Electrocardiogram is used to assess the electrical activity and cardiac rhythm of the heart.
Option 4:Echocardiogram is used to study the structure, size, and function of the heart. It also identifies the problems in the chambers of the heart.

optionList.content: Switching from whole to skim milk
Switching from whole to skim milk

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse provides nutritional counseling to a client related to avoiding consumption of excessive fat in the diet. During a follow-up visit, thethe client has total cholesterol of 340 mg/dL and low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol of 170 mg/dL. The nurse anticipates which action to be responsible for this lab result?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The normal levels of total cholesterol and low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol should be less than 200 mg/dL and 150 mg/dL, respectively. The total cholesterol value of 340 mg/dL and LDL cholesterol of 170 mg/dL indicate hypercholesterolemia in the client. Potato chips contain high amounts of fat; therefore, the client should avoid potato chips in his or her diet.
Option 2:Whole milk contains a high amount of fat. Therefore, a client with hypercholesterolemia should avoid consuming whole milk.
Option 3:Oranges contain no fat. Therefore, a client with hypercholesterolemia can consume two oranges per day.
Option 4:Shredded cheeses contain high amounts of fat. Therefore, a client with hypercholesterolemia should not consume shredded cheeses.

studentQuestionArray.content: Which descriptor of judgment does the nurse use to describe a physically handicapped client?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The nurse uses the descriptor "disabled" for a handicapped client.
Option 2:The nurse will use the descriptor "complicated" if the case is complex.
Option 3:The nurse will use the descriptor "disproportionate" if it is too large or too small in comparison with a norm.
Option 4:The term “crippled” is no longer used to describe a person who is disabled.

studentQuestionArray.content: Which diagnostic test measures the speed at which red blood cells settle in a solution?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Bone biopsies are done to diagnose tumors or infection of the bone.
Option 2:CRP measures a protein in the blood that is released when an infection is present.
Option 3:Rheumatoid factor measures the body's autoimmune response to an antigen.
Option 4:ESR measures the speed at which red blood cells settle in a solution.

studentQuestionArray.content: Which diagnostic test provides direct visualization of the upper gastrointestinal system?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Sigmoidoscopy is used for visualizing the anal canal, rectum, and sigmoid colon.
Option 2:Fiberoptic colonoscopy test aids in direct visualization of the rectum, colon, entire large intestine, and distal small bowel.
Option 3:Barium enema is used to examine the rectum, colon, and distal small bowel.
Option 4:Esophagogastroduodenoscopy aids in direct visualization of the upper gastrointestinal system.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client is experiencing symptoms of diabetes mellitus. Which laboratory test helps in diagnosing this disorder?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Diabetes mellitus is a condition of deficiency of insulin or resistance to the existing insulin in the body. This results in high blood glucose levels. Therefore, in order to diagnose diabetes mellitus, the nurse has to check the blood glucose levels in the client.
Option 2:Creatinine test is used to assess the kidney function but not diabetes mellitus.
Option 3:Total iron binding capacity indicates iron status of the body. It is performed to diagnose anemia in the client.
Option 4:Albumin test is used to diagnose liver disease and nutritional problems but not diabetes mellitus.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client complains of difficulty in breathing. After examination, the nurse concludes that the client has ineffective airway clearance and is anxious. The nurse decides to first treat the client for ineffective airway clearance. Which type of problem urgency does the nurse assign for the problem of ineffective airway clearance in the client?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Low priority is given for problems that require minimal supporting nursing interventions. Therefore, clients with mild anxiety are given low priority.
Option 2:High priority is given for problems that are life- threatening, such as ineffective airway clearance. Therefore, the nurse gives high priority to the life-threatening problem in the client.
Option 3:Medium priority urgency is given for problems that are destructive but not life threatening.
Option 4:Patient preference is giving high priority to problems, which the client feels important.

studentQuestionArray.content: After reviewing the urinalysis report of a malnourished client, the nurse suspects that the client has pernicious anemia. The presence of which finding supports the nurse's conclusion?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Protein in the urine sample indicates renal disease.
Option 2:Ketones in the urine sample indicate diabetes mellitus.
Option 3:Hemoglobin in the urine sample indicates renal trauma or nephrolithiasis.
Option 4:Urobilinogen is a byproduct formed by the metabolism of bilirubin. Deficiency of vitamin B12 causes pernicious anemia and increases the level of urobilinogen in the urine.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client complains of sore throat and difficulty swallowing. Upon assessment, the nurse concludes that the client has enlarged adenoids and mouth-breathing with halitosis. The serum analysis report indicates the presence of leukocytosis. The client has symptoms of which disease process?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Sore throat, difficulty swallowing, enlarged adenoids, and mouth-breathing with halitosis indicate tonsillitis. Tonsillitis is caused by a bacterial infection that increases white blood cell count.
Option 2:Influenza is a viral infection associated with fever, headache, chills, erythematous rash, and diarrhea.
Option 3:Laryngomalacia is a congenital disorder characterized by abnormal laryngeal cartilage, mild tachypnea, and a crowing noise with respirations.
Option 4:Tracheoesophageal fistula is associated with cyanosis, coughing spells, and abdominal distention.

optionList.content: Computed tomography (CT) scan of the brain
Computed tomography (CT) scan of the brain

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is caring for a child with ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt catheter who developed papilledema, double vision, and sunsetting eyes. Which diagnostic tests does the nurse anticipate to be prescribed for the child? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:A sweat test helps determine the amount of sweat produced by the sweat glands to diagnose cystic fibrosis.
Option 2:Papilledema, double vision, and sunsetting eyes are the clinical manifestations of hydrocephalus. If a client with ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt catheter has symptoms of hydrocephalus, the health-care provider will prescribe a CXR to help determine the patency and functioning of the shunt catheters.
Option 3:An abdomen x-ray is used to visualize the abdominal organs and help determine the patency and functioning of the shunt catheters.
Option 4:Polysomnography is used to diagnose sleep disorders. It does not help determine the patency and functioning of the shunt catheters.
Option 5:CT scan of the brain helps determine the functioning of the brain and patency of the shunt catheters.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is caring for a client who has prolonged wound healing. Which laboratory parameters does the nurse anticipate will be assessed? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1: A low red blood cell count could indicate bleeding.
Option 2:White blood cells help fight against foreign substances. The white blood cell count increases if a client has a wound or infection. When there is a decrease in the level of white blood cells, wound healing is prolonged. Therefore, when wound healing is prolonged, the nurse should assess for white blood cell count.
Option 3:Wound healing is part of protein synthesis. Low levels of serum protein prolong wound healing. Therefore, when wound healing is prolonged, the nurse should assess for serum protein.
Option 4:Prothrombin time helps calculate clotting time. Prothrombin time is assessed in the event of prolonged blood clotting. Assessing prothrombin time does not provide information on delayed wound healing.
Option 5:Decreased albumin is associated with insufficient protein, which, in turn, delays wound healing. Therefore, the serum albumin level is measured when there is a prolonged wound healing.
